claimText: A research team under the Department of Science and Technology (DST) has developed a Sodium-Ion Battery that can charge up to 80 percent in six minutes and lasts for over 3000 charge cycles.
evidenceExcerpt: Developed by a DST team: charges to 80% in 6 minutes, lasts over 3000 charge cycles.
examUse: Statement-based question on recent indigenous technological advancements in energy storage.
distractorHooks: Attributing the development to a foreign entity or private corporation instead of DST
Altering the charge time to 60 minutes or the capacity to 100 percent
Stating the battery lasts for less than 1000 cycles

claimText: Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs) utilize sodium ions (Na+) instead of lithium ions (Li+) to carry electrical charge.
evidenceExcerpt: Uses sodium ions (Na+) to carry charge instead of lithium ions (Li+).
examUse: Basic mechanism question or correct-pair matching for battery technologies.
distractorHooks: Claiming SIBs use a combination of lithium and sodium ions
Stating SIBs use potassium or solid-state lithium for charge transport

claimText: Sodium-Ion Batteries generally have a lower energy density when compared to Lithium-ion batteries.
evidenceExcerpt: Has lower energy density compared to Lithium-ion batteries.
examUse: Comparison question evaluating the trade-offs between Sodium-ion and Lithium-ion batteries.
distractorHooks: Stating Sodium-Ion Batteries have a higher energy density than Lithium-ion batteries
Claiming the energy density of SIBs and Li-ion batteries is identical

claimText: Sodium-Ion Batteries can be transported at zero voltage, which provides a higher safety profile by reducing fire risks.
evidenceExcerpt: Higher safety profile: can be transported at zero voltage (fully discharged), which reduces fire risks.
examUse: Statement-based question on the advantages and safety features of Sodium-Ion Batteries.
distractorHooks: Stating SIBs must be transported partially or fully charged to maintain battery health
Claiming SIBs have a higher fire risk during transport compared to Lithium-ion batteries
